chapters.title: What a Business Analyst actually does
chapters.lead: Ask five people what a Business AnalystThe person who translates business needs into requirements a delivery team can build.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short

A BA elicits, documents, and validates — sitting between business needs and the delivery team, translating vague asks into precise, buildable requirements.

Groundwork · Chapter 00

What a Business Analyst actually does

Ask five people what a Business AnalystThe person who translates business needs into requirements a delivery team can build.
press Enter or click to read more does and you will get five different answers. Here is the honest version: a BA makes sure the right thing gets built, by finding out what people actually need before anyone writes a line of code.

Every product decision starts as a vague idea somewhere in the business. "Customers keep asking where their order is." "Support is overwhelmed on Friday nights." A BA's job is to take that vague, half-formed thought and turn it into something a designer can design and a developer can build, without losing what was actually meant.

The dhaba order version
Picture a busy dhaba on a Friday night. A customer walks up and says, "give me something filling, not too spicy, quick." A good waiter does not shout that straight into the kitchen. They ask a follow-up or two, translate it into something precise — "one plain paratha, medium plate, no green chilli, priority ticket" — and hand the kitchen a ticket they can actually cook from. A BA is that waiter, standing between a business that knows what it wants only vaguely, and a team that needs something precise to build.
0.1

The three things a BA is really doing

Underneath all the job titles and diagrams, a BA's day-to-day work comes down to three things, always in this order: find out what is actually needed (not just what was first said), write it down so nobody has to guess, and make sure what gets delivered actually matches what was written down.

The BA loop
Elicittalk to the right people and dig past the first answer
Documentwrite it down precisely enough that two people would build the same thing
Validatecheck the delivered work actually solves the original problem

Everything in this course is really a deeper look at one of those three steps.

0.2

Where a BA sits

Requirements flow one way, questions and clarifications flow back the other way. A BA who only passes messages along without questioning them is not really doing the job; the value is in the translation, not the relay.

Common trip-ups
  • Confusing BA with Project Manager. A PM manages timelines, budget, and people. A BA manages what is actually being built and why. The same person sometimes does both, but they are different jobs.
  • Writing down the first thing you are told. The first sentence a stakeholder says is rarely the full requirement. Chapter 02 covers how to dig past it.
  • Disappearing after the requirements doc is written. A BA stays involved through delivery, answering questions and checking the built thing against what was asked for.
0.3

Recap

A BA elicits, documents, and validates. They sit between people who need something and people who build it, and their real job is translation, not transcription. Every chapter from here builds one part of that loop.

Easy
A stakeholder says "the app is too slow." What should a BA do first?
Easy
Which best describes the difference between a BA and a Project Manager?
Medium
TastyGo's support lead says: "customers keep messaging us asking where their order is." Write two or three follow-up questions you would ask before writing any requirement.

chapters.title: The BA toolkit landscape
chapters.lead: You do not need to master a dozen tools before you start. You need to know what each one is actually for, so you reach for the right one when the moment comes.
chapters.html:
In short

JIRA/Azure DevOps for tracking, Confluence/Notion for documents, Excel for matrices, Visio/draw.io for diagrams, SQL for answering real data questions directly.

Groundwork · Chapter 0b

The BA toolkit landscape

You do not need to master a dozen tools before you start. You need to know what each one is actually for, so you reach for the right one when the moment comes.

The everyday toolkit
JIRA / Azure DevOpstracking requirements as tickets, linking them to delivery work
Confluence / Notionwriting and sharing BRDs, FRDs, and meeting notes
Excel / Google SheetsRACI matrices, traceability matrices, quick data checks
Visio / Lucidchart / draw.ioprocess maps, BPMN diagrams, wireframe sketches
SQLanswering "how often does this actually happen?" straight from real data

That last one is why SQLingo pairs so well with this course. A BA who can write SELECT and GROUP BY can answer "how many orders were affected last month" in minutes, instead of waiting days for someone else to pull the number.

The chai-table version
Think of these tools like the equipment behind a chai stall: the kettle (JIRA) keeps track of what is brewing and in what order, the notebook (Confluence) is where the recipe and daily notes live, and the scale (SQL, Excel) is what you weigh things on when someone asks "are we sure?" None of it replaces knowing what a good cup of chai actually needs, it just helps you deliver it consistently.
Common trip-ups
  • Tool-hopping instead of communicating. A perfectly organized JIRA board does not replace an actual conversation with a confused stakeholder.
  • Treating diagrams as decoration. A process map exists to be checked against reality, not to look polished in a slide deck.
0.1

Recap

Tools support the three BA activities from chapter 00, they do not replace them. Pick the lightest tool that gets the job done clearly.

Easy
Which tool would you reach for to answer "how many refund requests did we get last week, exactly?"
Easy
A BRD (requirements document) most naturally lives in:

chapters.title: Meet the company
chapters.lead: Every example in this course happens inside one company, so the scenarios build on each other instead of starting from zero every chapter. If you have been through SQLingo, this will look familiar.
chapters.html:
In short

TastyGo: a food-delivery company (customers, restaurants, orders) making money on commission. Recurring stakeholders: Ops Head, Support Lead, Engineering Manager, Marketing Lead.

Groundwork · Chapter 0i

Meet the company

Every example in this course happens inside one company, so the scenarios build on each other instead of starting from zero every chapter. If you have been through SQLingo, this will look familiar.

TastyGo is a food delivery app operating across Mumbai, Delhi, Pune, and Bangalore. It connects customers to restaurants, and every order placed links exactly one customer to exactly one restaurant. This is the same customers/restaurants/orders world used throughout SQLingo, just seen now from a business angle instead of a query one.

Who you'll meet in this course
Ops Headowns delivery times, restaurant onboarding, day-to-day operations
Support Leadowns the customer support team and ticket queue
Engineering Managerowns the delivery team that actually builds features
Marketing Leadowns promotions, loyalty programs, customer communication

TastyGo makes money by taking a commission on every order. That single fact explains almost every decision the business makes: anything that increases the number of orders, or the average order size, is worth investing in; anything that increases refunds or cancelled orders eats directly into revenue.

Why this matters
A requirement never exists in isolation. When the Support Lead later asks for a "better refund process," a good BA already knows refunds cost the business money directly, so the real requirement is not just "make refunds easier," it is "make refunds fast enough to keep customers happy, without making them so easy that people abuse the system."
0.1

Recap

TastyGo, its four recurring stakeholders, and its commission-based business model are the backdrop for every scenario from here on. Keep this chapter in mind; later chapters will assume you remember who the Ops Head, Support Lead, Engineering Manager, and Marketing Lead are.

Easy
Why does TastyGo's commission-based business model matter to a BA?

chapters.title: Stakeholder identification & RACI
chapters.lead: Before you can gather requirements, you need to know whose requirements actually count, and what each person is supposed to do about the decision at hand.
chapters.html:
In short
RACI: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome, exactly one), Consulted (input before), Informed (told after).
Part I · Chapter 01

Stakeholder identification & RACI

Before you can gather requirements, you need to know whose requirements actually count, and what each person is supposed to do about the decision at hand.

A stakeholderAnyone with an interest in, or influence over, the outcome.
press Enter or click to read more is not just "someone in a meeting." It is anyone whose work changes, or whose approval is needed, because of what you are building. Missing a real stakeholder is one of the most common reasons a delivered feature gets rejected late, after it is already built.

The chai-table version
Imagine reorganizing seating at a busy dhaba. The obvious stakeholder is the owner. But the waiters need to be asked (their walking routes change), the regular customers need to be considered (their favourite table might move), and the kitchen staff care too (a bigger dining area might mean more orders at once). Forgetting the waiters is exactly how a "great idea" turns into chaos on day one.
1.1

The RACI matrix

Once you know who the stakeholders are, RACIA grid that assigns Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed to each task.
press Enter or click to read more tells you exactly what role each one plays on a given task or decision.

The four roles
Responsibleactually does the work
Accountableowns the outcome, signs off — exactly one person per task
Consultedgives input before the decision is made, two-way conversation
Informedtold after the fact, one-way notification
1.2

Worked example: live order tracking at TastyGo

TastyGo is adding a live order-tracking screen. Here is a RACI for the decision to launch it:

Only the Ops Head is Accountable, one clear owner. Engineering is Responsible for building it. Support is Consulted because they will field questions about it. Marketing is only Informed, since they need to know it exists but have no say in how it works.

Common trip-ups
  • More than one Accountable. If two people are both marked A, nobody is really accountable. Pick one.
  • Everyone marked Consulted. If everyone must weigh in on everything, decisions never get made. Be selective.
  • Confusing Responsible with Accountable. The person who does the work is not always the person who owns whether it succeeds.
1.3

Recap

Identify every stakeholder whose work or approval is affected, then use RACI to make each person's role explicit: exactly one Accountable, the doers as Responsible, a short list of Consulted, and everyone else Informed.

Easy
In a RACI matrix, how many people should typically be marked "Accountable" for a single task?
Medium
TastyGo's Support Lead will field customer questions about a new feature but has no say in how it is built. What should they be marked as in the RACI?
Medium
TastyGo is deciding whether to let customers cancel an order after it has been accepted by the restaurant. List the stakeholders you would include, and assign each one a RACI role, with a one-line reason.

chapters.title: Needs vs. wants (root cause thinking)
chapters.lead: What a stakeholder asks for first is almost never the actual requirement. It is their guess at a solution to a problem they have not fully explained yet.
chapters.html:
In short
5 Whys: keep asking "why" 3-5 times past the first stated want, until you reach an actual, fixable root cause.
Part I · Chapter 02

Needs vs. wants (root cause thinking)

What a stakeholder asks for first is almost never the actual requirement. It is their guess at a solution to a problem they have not fully explained yet.

A "want" is the solution someone shows up with. A "need" is the underlying problem that solution is trying to solve. A BA's job is to hear the want, and politely dig until the real need surfaces, because the same need can often be solved more cheaply, or more completely, by a different solution than the one first suggested.

The chai-table version
A regular customer tells the dhaba owner, "put a TV on that wall." That is a want, a specific solution. Dig one level and the actual need might be "I get bored waiting 15 minutes for my order." A TV solves that. So does a faster kitchen, a token system so people know their wait time, or free roasted peanuts while they wait. The TV was just the first idea that came to the customer's mind.
2.1

The 5 Whys

The simplest root-causeThe real, underlying reason a problem is happening, not its symptom.
press Enter or click to read more technique is also the most reliable: keep asking "why" until the answer stops being useful, usually three to five times.

The root cause turned out to be a missing feature, not a support-team training problem or a slow kitchen. That changes what gets built entirely.

Common trip-ups
  • Stopping at the first "why." One level down is usually still a symptom, not a cause.
  • Jumping straight to a solution. "Let's add push notifications" might be right, but only after the actual need is confirmed.
  • Treating every "why" as someone's fault. Root-cause analysis is about the process, not about blaming a person.
2.2

Recap

Wants are solutions people bring you. Needs are the problems underneath. Use the 5 Whys to walk from one to the other before writing any requirement down.

Easy
A stakeholder says "add a filter button to the menu." This statement is best described as a:
Medium
What is the main risk of writing down the first thing a stakeholder asks for, without digging further?
Medium
TastyGo's Marketing Lead says, "we need a bigger discount banner on the home screen, ours is too small." Write a short 5 Whys chain (3-4 steps) to find the real need.

chapters.title: As-is vs. to-be analysis
chapters.lead: You cannot design a better process for something you have not accurately mapped as it exists today. Skipping this step is how "improvements" quietly break things nobody realized still depended on the old way.
chapters.html:
In short
Map the as-is (how it works today) before designing the to-be (the improved version) — target only the documented bottleneck.
Part I · Chapter 03

As-is vs. to-be analysis

You cannot design a better process for something you have not accurately mapped as it exists today. Skipping this step is how "improvements" quietly break things nobody realized still depended on the old way.

The as-isHow a process works today, before any change.
press Enter or click to read more state is a plain, factual map of how a process works right now, warts and all. The to-beHow a process should work after the proposed change.
press Enter or click to read more state is the redesigned version, once a specific, documented problem in the as-is has been addressed.

3.1

Worked example: TastyGo refunds

Today, a refund at TastyGo works like this:

The as-is map makes the actual bottleneck obvious: two manual, human steps in the middle. The proposed to-be state targets exactly those, and nothing else:

Common trip-ups
  • Designing the to-be before mapping the as-is. You end up "fixing" a step that was never actually the problem.
  • Mapping the as-is from memory instead of observation. What people say they do and what they actually do often differ.
  • Redesigning everything at once. The best to-be states change as little as possible while still solving the documented problem.
3.2

Recap

Map the as-is honestly first. Let it show you exactly where the real bottleneck is. Then design a to-be state that targets that bottleneck specifically, not the whole process.

Easy
What is the main purpose of documenting the as-is state before proposing changes?
Medium
In the TastyGo refund example, which as-is steps did the to-be design actually target?
Hard
TastyGo's Ops Head wants to redesign how new restaurants get onboarded onto the platform. Before proposing a to-be process, list 3-4 questions you would ask to map the current as-is state.

chapters.title: SWOT & feasibility basics
chapters.lead: Before any idea becomes a requirement, it is worth a quick sanity check: is this actually a good idea, and can it realistically be delivered?
chapters.html:
In short
SWOT: Strengths, Weaknesses (internal) / Opportunities, Threats (external). Feasibility: Technical, Operational, Financial, Schedule.
Part I · Chapter 04

SWOT & feasibility basics

Before any idea becomes a requirement, it is worth a quick sanity check: is this actually a good idea, and can it realistically be delivered?

SWOT organizes your thinking about an idea into four honest categories. It is only useful when the answers are specific to the actual decision, not generic statements that could apply to any company.

Notice each box above is specific to TastyGo considering a loyalty points feature, not a generic statement. That specificity is what makes SWOT useful instead of a box-ticking exercise.

4.1

Feasibility: four questions

Before committing to an idea, check
Technicalcan it actually be built with the systems and skills available?
Operationalcan the business realistically run it day to day once live?
Financialdoes the expected benefit outweigh the cost to build and run it?
Schedulecan it be delivered inside the timeframe that actually matters?
Common trip-ups
  • Generic SWOT entries. "Strong brand" tells you nothing useful about this specific decision.
  • Skipping feasibility because an idea sounds good. Enthusiasm is not the same as being buildable on time and on budget.
4.2

Recap

SWOT forces an honest, specific look at an idea from four angles. Feasibility then checks whether it can realistically be delivered, across technical, operational, financial, and schedule dimensions.

Easy
Which SWOT category would "a competitor already has a well-known loyalty program" belong in?
Medium
A feature is technically easy to build but would require support agents to learn a completely new tool overnight. Which feasibility dimension does this mainly concern?
Medium
TastyGo is considering letting customers schedule orders up to 24 hours in advance. Write one specific point for each SWOT category.

chapters.title: Elicitation techniques
chapters.lead: ElicitationThe set of techniques used to draw requirements out of stakeholders.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Elicitation toolbox: interviews (depth), workshops (group agreement), surveys (breadth), observation (say vs. do), document analysis (already half-written).
Part II · Chapter 05

Elicitation techniques

ElicitationThe set of techniques used to draw requirements out of stakeholders.
press Enter or click to read more is not one thing, it is a toolbox. Picking the wrong technique for the situation is one of the quietest ways a BA ends up with weak requirements.

Match the technique to the situation
1:1 interviewdeep, sensitive, or conflicting topics with one stakeholder
Workshopseveral stakeholders need to agree on something together
Surveya broad, shallow read on many people's opinions quickly
Observationwatching how a process is actually done, not how people describe it
Document analysisthe process is already written down somewhere, just outdated

At TastyGo, if the Support Lead and Ops Head disagree about how refunds should work, a 1:1 with each first (to hear the honest version without the other person in the room) usually surfaces more than jumping straight to a joint workshop.

The chai-table version
You would not survey a hundred customers to find out why one specific regular stopped coming in, and you would not sit down for a one-on-one chat to understand what snacks a whole neighbourhood wants. The technique has to match the size and shape of the question.
Common trip-ups
  • Using only one technique for everything. Interviews alone will miss what a stakeholder does not think to mention but would do differently in practice.
  • Trusting what people say over what they do. Observation regularly contradicts what stakeholders describe in interviews, not out of dishonesty, just habit blindness.
5.1

Recap

Elicitation is a toolbox, not a single method. Pick interviews for depth, workshops for group agreement, surveys for breadth, observation for the gap between what people say and do, and document analysis when something is already half-written.

Easy
You need three department heads to agree on one shared process by the end of the meeting. Which technique fits best?
Medium
Support agents say refunds "usually" take one day, but you suspect it is longer. What is the most reliable way to check?
Medium
TastyGo's Marketing Lead wants quick, broad input from customers on whether they would use a loyalty program, without a long back-and-forth. Which technique would you choose, and why?

chapters.title: Running a requirements workshop
chapters.lead: A workshop without structure turns into a meeting where the loudest person's opinion becomes the requirement. A little preparation fixes that.
chapters.html:
In short
Workshops: send the agenda ahead, timebox topics and use a parking lot for tangents, send written notes right after.
Part II · Chapter 06

Running a requirements workshop

A workshop without structure turns into a meeting where the loudest person's opinion becomes the requirement. A little preparation fixes that.

Before, during, after
Beforesend a clear agenda and the specific decision you need, in advance
Duringtimebox each topic, keep an open "parking lot" for off-topic points
Aftersend written notes and decisions within a day, while memory is fresh

The "parking lot" matters more than it sounds. When TastyGo's Ops Head starts raising a delivery-partner pay question during a refund-policy workshop, writing it on the parking lot instead of debating it keeps the meeting on track without dismissing the concern.

Common trip-ups
  • No agenda sent beforehand. People arrive unprepared and the meeting drifts.
  • One person dominating. A facilitator's job includes actively inviting quieter stakeholders to speak.
  • No written follow-up. A decision that only exists in people's memory gets re-litigated later.
6.1

Recap

Send the agenda ahead of time, timebox topics and park distractions during the workshop, and send written notes immediately after. All three matter; skipping any one of them quietly weakens the outcome.

Easy
What is a "parking lot" used for in a workshop?
Medium
Why send a workshop agenda in advance rather than at the start of the meeting?
Medium
During a TastyGo workshop about the refund policy, the Engineering Manager keeps steering the conversation toward an unrelated database migration. As facilitator, what would you do?

chapters.title: Writing good interview questions
chapters.lead: The way a question is phrased quietly decides the answer you get. A leading question gets you agreement, not truth.
chapters.html:
In short
Prefer open questions over leading ones ("walk me through..." not "don't you think..."). Ask one thing at a time. Use "why" and "how often" follow-ups.
Part II · Chapter 07

Writing good interview questions

The way a question is phrased quietly decides the answer you get. A leading question gets you agreement, not truth.

Open vs. leading
Leading"Don't you think the checkout is too slow?" — invites agreement, not honesty
Open"Walk me through what happens when you check out." — invites the real story

Follow-up prompts do most of the real work in an interview: "why did that happen," "how often does that come up," "what do you do when that happens." These push past the first, polished answer into what actually happens day to day.

The chai-table version
Asking a regular "you like the extra ginger in your chai, right?" gets a polite yes even if it is not quite true. Asking "how would you want your chai different, if at all?" gets the real answer, because it does not assume what they will say.
Common trip-ups
  • Stacking two questions into one. "Is the app slow and confusing?" forces one answer onto two different issues.
  • Using internal jargon the stakeholder does not use. Ask about their world in their words, not your team's vocabulary.
7.1

Recap

Prefer open questions over leading ones, ask one thing at a time, and use "why" and "how often" follow-ups to get past the first, tidy answer.

Easy
Which of these is a leading question?
Medium
A stakeholder says "the app is slow and confusing." What is the best follow-up?
Medium
Rewrite this leading question into an open one: "Don't you think restaurants should be onboarded faster?"

chapters.title: Handling conflicting stakeholder asks
chapters.lead: Two reasonable stakeholders can want opposite things for equally good reasons. Picking a side quietly, or refusing to pick at all, both fail the project.
chapters.html:
In short
Conflicting stakeholders: name the conflict openly, find the shared underlying goal, escalate with a specific recommendation, not just the problem.
Part II · Chapter 08

Handling conflicting stakeholder asks

Two reasonable stakeholders can want opposite things for equally good reasons. Picking a side quietly, or refusing to pick at all, both fail the project.

At TastyGo, Marketing wants refunds to be instant and generous, to keep customers happy. Finance wants refunds reviewed carefully, since instant refunds have been abused before. Neither is wrong, they are optimizing for different things.

A repeatable way through it
Name itsay the conflict out loud to both sides, do not let it stay unspoken
Find the shared goalboth sides usually want "happy customers who do not cost us money"
Escalate with a recommendationif it cannot be resolved at your level, bring it up with a proposed answer, not just the problem

A workable middle ground here: instant refunds under a small amount, manual review above it. Neither side gets everything, but both underlying goals are respected.

Common trip-ups
  • Silently picking the side of whoever asked last, or is more senior. This erodes trust once the other stakeholder finds out.
  • Escalating without a recommendation. "They disagree, please decide" is far less useful than "here are two options and which I would pick, and why."
8.1

Recap

Name the conflict explicitly, look for the shared goal underneath the disagreement, and if you escalate, escalate with a specific recommendation rather than just the problem.

Easy
Two stakeholders want opposite things. What should a BA generally avoid doing?
Medium
When escalating an unresolved conflict to leadership, what makes the escalation more useful?
Hard
TastyGo's Ops Head wants restaurant onboarding approved in 24 hours to grow supply fast. The Support Lead wants a thorough 5-day review to avoid onboarding unreliable restaurants that generate complaints. Propose a resolution.

chapters.title: The Business Requirements Document (BRD)
chapters.lead: A BRDBusiness Requirements Document: the what and why, in business language.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
BRD sections: Purpose & background, Business objectives, Scope & out of scope, Requirements, Assumptions & sign-off. Business language, not technical.
Part III · Chapter 09

The Business Requirements Document (BRD)

A BRDBusiness Requirements Document: the what and why, in business language.
press Enter or click to read more is the document a business stakeholder actually reads and signs off on. It has to be precise without being technical.

A BRD's usual sections
Purpose & backgroundwhy this project exists, what problem prompted it
Business objectivesthe measurable outcome this should achieve
Scope & out of scopewhat is included, and just as important, what is explicitly not
Requirementsthe numbered list of business needs, in business language
Assumptions & sign-offwhat is being taken for granted, and who formally approves it

For TastyGo's automated refund project, a BRD objective would read "reduce average refund turnaround from 3 days to under 1 hour," not "build an auto-refund microservice," which belongs in the FRD instead.

Common trip-ups
  • Writing in system/technical language. A BRD that a business stakeholder cannot fully read and approve has failed its one job.
  • No explicit out-of-scope section. What is left unsaid gets assumed differently by different readers.
  • No named approver. A BRD nobody signed off on is not really approved, whatever anyone believes.
9.1

Recap

A BRD states the business problem, objective, and scope in language a non-technical stakeholder can read and approve, with a named sign-off. It says what and why, leaving how to the FRD.

Easy
A BRD should primarily be written in:
Medium
Why does a BRD need an explicit "out of scope" section?
Medium
Write one business objective (not a technical solution) for TastyGo's "let customers cancel an order after acceptance" project.

chapters.title: The Functional Requirements Document (FRD)
chapters.lead: Where a BRD says what and why, an FRDFunctional Requirements Document: the detailed, system-level how.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
FRD turns an approved BRD into specific, testable system rules. If a rule can't become a pass/fail test case, it isn't precise enough yet.
Part III · Chapter 10

The Functional Requirements Document (FRD)

Where a BRD says what and why, an FRDFunctional Requirements Document: the detailed, system-level how.
press Enter or click to read more says exactly how the system should behave, precisely enough that two different developers would build the same thing.

Take the BRD objective from the last chapter ("reduce refund turnaround to under 1 hour"). An FRD turns that into specific, testable rules:

Example FRD excerpt: auto-refund eligibility
Rule 1orders under ₹500 with no prior refund this month auto-approve instantly
Rule 2orders ₹500 and above route to manual review within 1 business hour
Rule 3a customer with 3+ refunds in 30 days always routes to manual review, regardless of amount

Notice each rule is precise enough to test directly: you can write a test case for exactly ₹499 vs. exactly ₹500, and for a customer's 3rd refund. Vague FRD language like "the system should handle refunds sensibly" cannot be tested at all.

Common trip-ups
  • Writing the FRD before the BRD is approved. Detailed rules for an unapproved business objective often get rewritten, wasting the detail work.
  • Vague, untestable wording. "Fast," "user-friendly," and "sensible" are not requirements until they have a number or a specific rule attached.
10.1

Recap

An FRD converts an approved business objective into specific, testable system rules. If a rule cannot be turned into a pass/fail test case, it is not precise enough yet.

Easy
Which of these is a properly specific FRD rule?
Medium
What is the main risk of writing detailed FRD rules before the BRD is signed off?
Hard
Turn this vague FRD line into a specific, testable rule: "The system should notify customers about delays."

chapters.title: User stories & acceptance criteria
chapters.lead: A user storyA short, plain-language statement of a need, from a user's point of view.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
User story: "As a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]." Acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then. Check quality with INVEST.
Part III · Chapter 11

User stories & acceptance criteria

A user storyA short, plain-language statement of a need, from a user's point of view.
press Enter or click to read more is the smallest useful unit of a requirement, written from the user's point of view. It only becomes buildable once paired with clear acceptance criteriaThe specific, testable conditions a user story must meet to be done.
press Enter or click to read more.

The shape of a user story
As a[role] — who wants this?
I want[goal] — what are they trying to do?
So that[benefit] — why does it matter to them?

Example, live order tracking: "As a customer, I want to see my order's live status, so that I don't have to message support to ask where it is."

11.1

Acceptance criteria, in Given/When/Then

Given a customer has placed an order When the restaurant marks it "out for delivery" Then the app shows "Out for delivery" with an updated ETA within 1 minute

Good acceptance criteria are specific enough that a tester and a developer would independently agree on whether the story is done, without asking anyone else.

INVEST: a quick quality check for a story
Independentdoes not depend on another unfinished story
Negotiabledetails can still be discussed, it is not a rigid spec
Valuabledelivers something a real user cares about on its own
Estimablethe team can roughly size how much work it is
Small & Testablefits in one sprint, and has clear pass/fail criteria
Common trip-ups
  • Stories too big to finish in a sprint. "As a customer, I want a great ordering experience" is a whole epic, not a story.
  • Missing acceptance criteria entirely. Without them, "done" means something different to everyone on the team.
11.2

Recap

A user story names who, what, and why in one sentence. Acceptance criteria, ideally in Given/When/Then form, make "done" objective rather than a matter of opinion.

Easy
Which part of "As a customer, I want live order tracking, so that I don't have to message support" is the benefit?
Medium
What does the "S" in INVEST stand for, and why does it matter?
Medium
Write a user story and two acceptance criteria for: "customers should be able to cancel an order within 2 minutes of placing it."

chapters.title: Use cases & use case diagrams
chapters.lead: A use caseA description of how an actor uses a system to achieve a goal.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Use case = actor + goal + main flow + alternate flows. Broader than a user story; alternate flows catch what the happy path misses.
Part III · Chapter 12

Use cases & use case diagrams

A use caseA description of how an actor uses a system to achieve a goal.
press Enter or click to read more is a bigger, more complete picture than a user story: not just the happy path, but every realistic way an interaction can go.

Use case: Track Order
Main flowcustomer opens order, sees current status and ETA
Alternate flowrestaurant has not updated status; app shows last known status with a timestamp
Alternate floworder is already delivered; app shows delivery confirmation instead of a live map

The alternate flows are exactly what a user story often leaves out. A use case forces you to think through what happens when the "normal" path does not hold.

Common trip-ups
  • Only documenting the happy path. Most production bugs live in the alternate flows nobody wrote down.
  • Confusing a use case with a user story. A story is a small, sprint-sized slice; a use case is the fuller picture a set of stories is often carved out of.
12.1

Recap

A use case names an actor, a goal, a main flow, and its alternate flows. It is broader than a single user story and is especially useful for surfacing edge cases before they become bugs.

Easy
In a use case diagram, what does the stick figure represent?
Medium
What is the main value of writing "alternate flows" for a use case?
Medium
For TastyGo's "Cancel Order" use case, write the main flow and one alternate flow.

chapters.title: Process mapping basics
chapters.lead: Before BPMN's full notation, it helps to get comfortable with the basic shapes every process map is built from.
chapters.html:
In short
Process map shapes: oval (start/end), rectangle (step), diamond (decision, 2+ paths out), arrow (sequence).
Part IV · Chapter 13

Process mapping basics

Before BPMN's full notation, it helps to get comfortable with the basic shapes every process map is built from.

The four basic shapes
Ovalstart or end of the process
Rectanglea step or activity someone performs
Diamonda decision point, with a different arrow for each possible answer
Arrowthe sequence, which step leads to which
Common trip-ups
  • No clear start or end. A map that just floats in the middle of a process is missing context.
  • Decisions with only one arrow out. A diamond needs at least two paths, or it is not really a decision.
13.1

Recap

Ovals for start/end, rectangles for steps, diamonds for decisions with multiple labelled paths out, arrows for sequence. Every more advanced notation (BPMN included) builds on these same four ideas.

Easy
In a basic process map, a diamond shape represents:
Easy
How many arrows should typically leave a decision diamond?
Medium
Describe, in words, a simple process map (start to end) for a customer leaving a rating after their TastyGo order is delivered.

chapters.title: BPMN fundamentals
chapters.lead: BPMNBusiness Process Model and Notation: a standard way to diagram processes.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
BPMN: pools/lanes (who), tasks (steps), gateways (exclusive = one path, parallel = all paths), events (start/end/timer).
Part IV · Chapter 14

BPMN fundamentals

BPMNBusiness Process Model and Notation: a standard way to diagram processes.
press Enter or click to read more adds one powerful idea on top of a basic process map: swimlanes, showing exactly who or what system does each step.

Core BPMN elements
Pool / lanewho or what performs the steps in that row
Taska rounded rectangle, one unit of work
Gatewaya diamond, splits or merges the flow based on a condition
Eventa circle, a start, end, or something that happens (like a timer)

The swimlanes above make the handoffs obvious at a glance: the customer only touches the first step, the system does the heavy lifting, and Support only gets involved for the review path, exactly the kind of clarity RACI gave you in chapter 01, now shown visually.

Common trip-ups
  • Every gateway drawn as the same type. An exclusive gateway (only one path taken) and a parallel gateway (all paths taken at once) mean very different things; mixing them up misrepresents the process.
  • Missing end events. Every path through the diagram should visibly terminate somewhere.
14.1

Recap

BPMN swimlanes show who performs each step; tasks, gateways, and events are its basic shapes. The visual handoff between lanes is often the most useful thing the diagram reveals.

Easy
What does a swimlane in a BPMN diagram represent?
Medium
What is the key difference between an exclusive gateway and a parallel gateway?
Hard
Describe, lane by lane, a BPMN-style flow for a restaurant marking an order "ready for pickup" and a delivery partner collecting it.

chapters.title: Data flow diagrams (DFD)
chapters.lead: A process map shows the order steps happen in. A DFD shows something different: where data comes from, what touches it, and where it ends up.
chapters.html:
In short
DFD building blocks: external entity (square), process (circle), data store (open rectangle), labelled data flow (arrow). Maps data movement, not sequence.
Part IV · Chapter 15

Data flow diagrams (DFD)

A process map shows the order steps happen in. A DFD shows something different: where data comes from, what touches it, and where it ends up.

DFD's four building blocks
External entitya square, something outside the system (a customer, a payment gateway)
Processa circle or rounded box, something that transforms data
Data storean open-ended rectangle, where data is held (a database table)
Data flowa labelled arrow, naming exactly what data moves, not just that something happens

Notice the DFD does not care about sequence or decisions at all, only what data moves where. That is exactly what makes it the wrong tool for showing "what happens if the restaurant is closed," and the right tool for spotting, say, that a process reads from a data store it was never given permission to touch.

Common trip-ups
  • Confusing a DFD with a process flow. A DFD has no decision diamonds and no explicit sequence; it is a map of data, not control flow.
  • Unlabelled arrows. An arrow that does not say what data is moving defeats the entire purpose of the diagram.
15.1

Recap

A DFD maps external entities, processes, and data stores, connected by labelled data flows. Use it to answer "where does this data come from and go to," not "what order do steps happen in."

Easy
What does a labelled arrow represent in a DFD?
Medium
Which question is a DFD best suited to answer?
Medium
Sketch (in words) a DFD for a customer submitting a rating: what external entity, process, and data store(s) would be involved?

chapters.title: ER modeling for BAs
chapters.lead: If you've been through SQLingo, this chapter is the same idea from the other direction: not writing queries against tables, but designing what those tables and their relationships should be in the first place.
chapters.html:
In short
ER modeling: entity (thing), attribute (property), relationship + cardinality (1:1, 1:M, M:M). M:M relationships usually need a junction entity.
Part IV · Chapter 16

ER modeling for BAs

If you've been through SQLingo, this chapter is the same idea from the other direction: not writing queries against tables, but designing what those tables and their relationships should be in the first place.

The three building blocks
Entitya thing worth tracking on its own (Customer, Restaurant, Order)
Attributea property of an entity (a customer's name, a restaurant's rating)
Relationship & cardinalityhow entities connect, and how many of one relate to how many of the other

This is exactly the customers/restaurants/orders schema from SQLingo, just drawn as a model instead of queried as tables. Order is a many-to-many junction between Customer and Restaurant precisely because a customer orders from many restaurants, and a restaurant serves many customers.

Common trip-ups
  • Modeling an attribute as its own entity. "City" is an attribute of Customer, not a separate entity, unless you actually need to track facts about cities themselves.
  • Skipping cardinality. "Customer relates to Order" is incomplete without saying one-to-many, which is what actually constrains the design.
16.1

Recap

Entities are the things worth tracking, attributes are their properties, and cardinality (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many) defines exactly how entities relate. A many-to-many relationship (like Customer to Restaurant) is usually resolved with a junction entity, like Order.

Easy
In ER modeling, what is an "attribute"?
Medium
Why is Order modeled as its own entity rather than just a link between Customer and Restaurant?
Hard
TastyGo wants to let restaurants respond to ratings with a reply. Describe the new entity you would add and its relationship to the existing Rating (or Order) entity.

chapters.title: Gap analysis
chapters.lead: Gap analysisComparing current capability to desired capability to find what must change.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Gap analysis: current state vs. specific, measurable desired state = the gap, which becomes the project's scope.
Part V · Chapter 17

Gap analysis

Gap analysisComparing current capability to desired capability to find what must change.
press Enter or click to read more is the as-is/to-be thinking from chapter 03, made explicit and structured enough to plan a project from.

Current → Desired → Gap
Current staterestaurants are onboarded manually over 5 days by one ops person
Desired statelow-risk restaurants onboard in under 24 hours, automatically
The gapno automated eligibility check exists yet; onboarding has no fast-track path

The gap itself is often the most useful output: it becomes almost directly the list of things the project actually needs to build, rather than a vague goal like "improve onboarding."

Common trip-ups
  • Vague desired state. "Onboarding should be better" cannot produce a specific gap. It needs a number or a concrete condition, like the 24-hour target above.
  • Skipping straight to solutions. The gap should describe what is missing, not yet how to build it, that comes after in the FRD.
17.1

Recap

Gap analysis states the current state and the desired state in specific, comparable terms, then names the concrete gap between them. That gap becomes the backbone of the project's scope.

Easy
What makes a desired state usable in a gap analysis?
Medium
The "gap" in a gap analysis should describe:
Medium
TastyGo currently has no way to flag repeat-refund customers. The desired state is catching abuse before a refund is auto-approved. Write the current state, desired state, and the gap.

chapters.title: Impact analysis & traceability matrix
chapters.lead: Before a change ships, two questions matter: what else does this touch, and can we prove every requirement actually got built and tested?
chapters.html:
In short
Impact analysis: what else does this change touch? Traceability matrix: requirement → design → build → test, nothing falls through.
Part V · Chapter 18

Impact analysis & traceability matrix

Before a change ships, two questions matter: what else does this touch, and can we prove every requirement actually got built and tested?

Impact analysis asks the first question. Automating refunds at TastyGo does not just touch the refund screen, it touches: the Finance reporting dashboard (refund totals now update instantly, not in daily batches), the Support team's tools (agents need to see auto-approved refunds too), and the restaurant payout calculation (a refunded order should not count toward a restaurant's earnings).

A small traceability matrixLinking each requirement to its design, build, and test, end to end.
press Enter or click to read more
REQ-04Auto-approve refunds under ₹500 → FRD Rule 1 → built in Sprint 12 → tested in TC-021
REQ-05Route refunds ₹500+ to review → FRD Rule 2 → built in Sprint 12 → tested in TC-022
REQ-06Flag repeat-refund customers → FRD Rule 3 → not yet built → no test case

REQ-06 falling through the cracks is exactly the kind of gap a traceability matrix catches before launch, instead of a customer or auditor catching it after.

Common trip-ups
  • Skipping impact analysis on "small" changes. Small changes to shared data are exactly what quietly break other teams' dashboards and reports.
  • Building a traceability matrix once and never updating it. It only protects you if it is kept current as requirements change.
18.1

Recap

Impact analysis maps what else a change touches beyond the obvious. A traceability matrix links every requirement to its design, build, and test, so nothing silently falls through.

Easy
What is the main purpose of a traceability matrix?
Medium
Why is impact analysis still necessary for a change that looks small and isolated?
Hard
TastyGo plans to let customers edit their delivery address after placing an order. List two areas beyond the ordering screen this change might impact.

chapters.title: Prioritization: MoSCoW & Kano
chapters.lead: Not every requirement deserves equal urgency. Two simple models keep prioritization from becoming "everything is a Must."
chapters.html:
In short
MoSCoW: Must / Should / Could / Won't (this release). Kano: Basic (prevents anger), Performance (scales with investment), Excitement (delights via surprise).
Part V · Chapter 19

Prioritization: MoSCoW & Kano

Not every requirement deserves equal urgency. Two simple models keep prioritization from becoming "everything is a Must."

MoSCoWA prioritization method: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have.
press Enter or click to read more, for one release
Must haveauto-approve small refunds — without this, the project fails its goal
Should haveflag repeat-refund customers — important, survivable if delayed one sprint
Could havea refund-status email, not just an in-app update — nice, not essential
Won't have (this release)letting customers choose store credit instead of a refund — explicitly deferred
19.1

Kano: not all features earn happiness equally

A basic feature (the app working at all) never makes anyone happy, it just avoids anger. A performance feature (faster delivery) makes people steadily happier the better it gets. An excitement feature (a surprise loyalty bonus) delights precisely because nobody expected it, but its absence is not noticed at all.

Common trip-ups
  • Marking everything "Must have." If nothing is Should/Could/Won't, MoSCoW has not actually done its job.
  • Investing heavily in basics expecting delight. A rock-solid basic feature stops complaints, it rarely generates enthusiasm on its own.
19.2

Recap

MoSCoW forces a release-level priority call on every requirement. Kano reminds you that not all features earn the same kind of satisfaction, basics prevent anger, performance features scale with investment, and excitement features delight through surprise.

Easy
In MoSCoW, which category is meant for something explicitly excluded from this release?
Medium
According to the Kano model, why doesn't investing more in a "basic" feature usually increase customer delight?
Medium
Classify these three TastyGo ideas using Kano: (1) the app not crashing during checkout, (2) faster average delivery time, (3) a free surprise dessert added occasionally to an order.

chapters.title: Data analysis for BAs
chapters.lead: Every technique in this course gets sharper with real numbers behind it. This is exactly where the SQLingo
chapters.html:
In short
Validate stakeholder claims against real data (GROUP BY / ORDER BY from SQLingo) before treating an impression as fact.
Part V · Chapter 20

Data analysis for BAs

Every technique in this course gets sharper with real numbers behind it. This is exactly where the SQLingo skillset pays off directly.

Suppose the Support Lead claims "most refund requests come from just a handful of repeat customers." Before treating that as fact, a BA who knows SQL can check it directly against TastyGo's own orders table:

SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS refund_count FROM orders WHERE rating_given IS NULL -- standing in for "flagged" orders here GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY refund_count DESC;

In minutes, that either confirms the claim (a few customers dominate the count) or quietly disproves it (refunds are actually spread evenly), and either answer changes what gets prioritized. This is the same GROUP BY and ORDER BY from SQLingo chapters 04 and 10, now used to settle a real business argument instead of a classroom exercise.

Where a BA reaches for real data
Validating a claim"most complaints are about X" — is that actually true?
Sizing a problemhow many customers, orders, or rupees does this actually affect?
Checking a fix workeddid the number actually move after the change shipped?
Common trip-ups
  • Presenting an anecdote as if it were data. "One customer complained loudly" is not the same as "this affects 40% of orders."
  • Not sanity-checking a surprising number. An unusually large or small result is often a data or query mistake before it is a real insight.
20.1

Recap

Wherever possible, replace a stakeholder's impression with an actual number pulled from real data. It is faster to be right, and far more persuasive when proposing a change.

Easy
Why is pulling real data preferable to relying on a stakeholder's general impression?
Medium
A query returns a surprisingly large number for "orders refunded this month." What should you do first?
Medium
Marketing claims "most customers who stop ordering do so after a bad rating experience." How would you use TastyGo's data to check this claim, in plain terms (no SQL needed)?

chapters.title: Agile fundamentals for BAs
chapters.lead: AgileAn iterative way of delivering software in small, frequently reviewed chunks.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Agile spreads BA work across the sprint: refine stories ahead of time, support planning, confirm delivered work at review against acceptance criteria.
Part VI · Chapter 21

Agile fundamentals for BAs

AgileAn iterative way of delivering software in small, frequently reviewed chunks.
press Enter or click to read more does not remove the need for BA work, it just changes its rhythm: from one big upfront document to a steady drip of refined, ready stories.

Where a BA fits into each ceremony
Backlog refinementwrite and clarify stories before they enter a sprint, ideally 1-2 sprints ahead
Sprint planninganswer the team's clarifying questions on the stories being pulled in
Sprint reviewconfirm the delivered work actually matches the acceptance criteria
Common trip-ups
  • Disappearing after backlog grooming. Questions come up mid-sprint; a BA who is unreachable slows the whole team down.
  • Treating "agile" as "no documentation." Agile favours lighter, just-in-time documentation, not none at all; acceptance criteria are still required.
21.1

Recap

Agile spreads BA work across the sprint cycle rather than front-loading it into one document: refine stories ahead of time, support planning, and confirm delivered work against acceptance criteria at review.

Easy
When should a BA typically refine and clarify upcoming stories?
Medium
What is a common misconception about BA work in Agile teams?
Medium
A developer asks a clarifying question about a story mid-sprint, and the BA who wrote it is on leave with no backup. What went wrong, and what would you do differently going forward?

chapters.title: Backlog management & grooming
chapters.lead: A backlogThe ordered list of everything that could be built next.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Definition of Ready (gate to enter a sprint) vs. Definition of Done (gate to call it finished). Split oversized stories by value, not by technical layer.
Part VI · Chapter 22

Backlog management & grooming

A backlogThe ordered list of everything that could be built next.
press Enter or click to read more that only grows and never gets pruned or refined stops being useful, it becomes a graveyard nobody trusts.

Definition of Ready vs. Definition of Done
Definition of Readya story has clear acceptance criteria, is sized, and has no unresolved blockers, before it enters a sprint
Definition of Donethe team agrees on exactly what "finished" means: coded, tested, reviewed, deployed

Grooming (or refinement) is where a BA earns their keep in this rhythm: splitting an oversized story ("redesign the whole checkout flow") into sized, independently valuable pieces ("add a promo code field," "show an order summary before payment"), each small enough to actually finish in a sprint, per the INVEST criteria from chapter 11.

Common trip-ups
  • Never pruning the backlog. Stories from a year ago that no longer matter should be archived, not left to clutter every grooming session.
  • Splitting a story badly. Splitting by technical layer ("frontend part," "backend part") usually creates two pieces neither of which delivers value alone; split by user-visible slices instead.
22.1

Recap

Definition of Ready gates what enters a sprint, Definition of Done gates what counts as finished. Regular grooming keeps the backlog sized, current, and trustworthy instead of an ever-growing pile.

Easy
"Definition of Ready" is checked:
Medium
What is usually wrong with splitting a story into "frontend part" and "backend part"?
Medium
Split this oversized story into two or three smaller, independently valuable stories: "As a customer, I want a completely redesigned checkout experience."

chapters.title: Working with UX
chapters.lead: A BA and a designer solve overlapping problems from different angles: the BA protects what the business needs, the designer protects how it feels to actually use.
chapters.html:
In short
Review wireframes against acceptance criteria and requirements; leave visual/styling calls to the designer.
Part VI · Chapter 23

Working with UX

A BA and a designer solve overlapping problems from different angles: the BA protects what the business needs, the designer protects how it feels to actually use.

A low-fidelity wireframe (boxes and labels, no colours or polish) is usually the right level of detail for a BA to review against requirements. It answers "does this screen include everything the acceptance criteria require," without getting pulled into a debate about button colour, which is the designer's call to make.

Good feedback vs. overstepping
Good BA feedback"the acceptance criteria requires showing the ETA, I don't see it in this wireframe"
Overstepping"I think this button should be blue instead of green"
Common trip-ups
  • Dictating visual design decisions. That is the designer's expertise, not the BA's role, unless there is a genuine business or accessibility requirement behind it.
  • Approving a wireframe without checking it against acceptance criteria. A beautiful screen that is missing a required field is still an incomplete requirement.
23.1

Recap

Review designs against acceptance criteria and business requirements, not personal visual taste. Give feedback on what is missing or wrong against the requirement, and leave visual craft decisions to the designer.

Easy
Which of these is appropriate BA feedback on a wireframe?
Medium
Why is a low-fidelity wireframe often the right level of detail for a BA to review?
Medium
A designer shows you a wireframe for the refund screen. Acceptance criteria require showing the refund amount and expected timing. The wireframe only shows the amount. What do you say?

chapters.title: UAT & sign-off
chapters.lead: UATUser Acceptance Testing: the business confirms the built thing is right.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
UAT test cases translate directly from acceptance criteria. Independent testers plus a named sign-off owner.
Part VI · Chapter 24

UAT & sign-off

UATUser Acceptance Testing: the business confirms the built thing is right.
press Enter or click to read more is the last checkpoint before something ships: not "does the code work," but "does this actually solve the business problem we started with."

UAT test cases come straight from acceptance criteria
Acceptance criterionGiven an order over ₹500, When a refund is requested, Then it routes to manual review
UAT test caseplace a ₹600 test order, request a refund, confirm it appears in the manual review queue, not auto-approved

Notice the test case is nearly a direct translation of the acceptance criterion. If a story's acceptance criteria are vague, UAT test cases become impossible to write precisely, one more reason chapter 11's Given/When/Then discipline pays off later.

Common trip-ups
  • UAT performed only by the people who wrote the requirements. Fresh eyes, ideally actual business users, catch what the requirement's own author has gone blind to.
  • No named sign-off owner. If nobody specific approves it, "UAT passed" is just an impression, not a decision anyone is accountable for.
24.1

Recap

UAT test cases should trace directly back to acceptance criteria. Ideally real business users perform it, and a named person formally signs off, closing the loop that started with the BRD's objective.

Easy
UAT test cases should be derived from:
Medium
Why is it best if UAT is not performed solely by the people who wrote the requirements?
Medium
Write one UAT test case for this acceptance criterion: "Given a customer has 3 or more refunds in 30 days, When they request a new refund of any amount, Then it is routed to manual review."

chapters.title: Stakeholder reporting
chapters.lead: A status update has one job: let a busy stakeholder know, in seconds, whether they need to act on anything right now.
chapters.html:
In short
RAG status: Green (on track), Amber (at risk, be aware), Red (blocked, needs action). Be honest, match detail to the audience.
Part VII · Chapter 25

Stakeholder reporting

A status update has one job: let a busy stakeholder know, in seconds, whether they need to act on anything right now.

RAG status, at a glance
Greenon track, nothing needed from the stakeholder
Amberat risk, stakeholder should be aware, may need a decision soon
Redblocked or off track, needs stakeholder action now

The auto-refund project, honestly reported: "Amber. Auto-approval logic is on track for Sprint 12. The repeat-refund detection story is blocked on a data question for Finance; need an answer by Thursday to stay on schedule." That single paragraph tells a busy Ops Head exactly what is fine, what is not, and what they specifically need to do.

Common trip-ups
  • Reporting everything as Green to avoid a hard conversation. This just delays the hard conversation to a worse moment, usually right before a deadline.
  • Too much technical detail for an executive audience. Match depth to the reader; a technical deep-dive belongs in a separate, optional appendix, not the headline status.
25.1

Recap

Use RAG status to communicate at-a-glance health, be honest about Amber and Red rather than defaulting to Green, and match the level of detail to the audience reading it.

Easy
What does a "Red" RAG status typically signal?
Medium
Why is marking a struggling project "Green" to avoid a difficult conversation harmful?
Medium
Write a short, honest status update for a TastyGo project that is one week behind because a third-party payment gateway integration is taking longer than expected.

chapters.title: Managing scope creep & change requests
chapters.lead: Scope creepSmall, unapproved additions that quietly grow a project beyond its plan.press Enter or click to read more
chapters.html:
In short
Treat every "small addition" as a change request: log it, assess impact, get an explicit approve/defer/reject decision.
Part VII · Chapter 26

Managing scope creep & change requests

Scope creepSmall, unapproved additions that quietly grow a project beyond its plan.
press Enter or click to read more rarely arrives as one big demand. It arrives as a dozen small, reasonable-sounding "while we're at it" additions.

"Since we're touching the refund screen anyway, can we also add a reason dropdown? And a refund history tab? And let support override the auto-approval manually?" Each request sounds small and reasonable alone. Together, they can double the size of the project no one ever formally approved.

A simple change-request process
Log itwrite the request down, do not just verbally agree in the moment
Assess impactwhat does this cost in time, and what does it put at risk?
Get an explicit decisionapproved now, deferred to next release, or rejected, decided by whoever owns that call
Common trip-ups
  • Silently absorbing small requests to avoid friction. Being agreeable in the moment is what causes deadlines to quietly slip later.
  • No visible change log. Without a record, nobody, including the requester, remembers what was added and why the timeline moved.
26.1

Recap

Treat every "small addition" as a real change request: log it, assess its actual impact, and get an explicit decision, rather than silently absorbing it and hoping the deadline still holds.

Easy
Scope creep most often happens through:
Medium
What is the risk of silently agreeing to a stakeholder's "quick addition" in the moment?
Medium
The Support Lead asks, mid-sprint, "can we also let agents manually re-trigger a failed refund while we're building this?" How do you respond?

chapters.title: Presenting findings
chapters.lead: A good BA presentation answers three things in order: what did you find, why does it matter, and what do you want the room to do about it.
chapters.html:
In short
Structure findings as Situation → So what → Recommendation. Lead with the headline and a specific ask for senior audiences.
Part VII · Chapter 27

Presenting findings

A good BA presentation answers three things in order: what did you find, why does it matter, and what do you want the room to do about it.

Situation → So what → Recommendation
Situationrefunds currently take 3 days on average, and cost the support team 40 hours a week
So whatthis delays customers, costs staff time, and is worse than two competitors' stated refund times
Recommendationautomate refunds under ₹500; expected to cut turnaround to under 1 hour and save ~30 support hours weekly

Notice the recommendation is specific and has a number attached, "we should improve refunds" gives an executive nothing to actually approve or reject.

Common trip-ups
  • Leading with detail instead of the headline. An executive audience wants the recommendation first, detail available if asked, not the reverse.
  • No clear ask. Every presentation to decision-makers should end with a specific thing you want them to approve, fund, or decide.
27.1

Recap

Structure findings as situation, so-what, and a specific recommendation. Lead with the headline for senior audiences, and always end with a clear, actionable ask.

Easy
In the "situation, so what, recommendation" structure, the "so what" explains:
Medium
For a senior executive audience with limited time, what is the best presentation structure?
Medium
Write a one-paragraph "situation, so what, recommendation" summary for the finding that 60% of support tickets are refund status questions.

chapters.title: Certifications & career paths
chapters.lead: A certification can open a door, especially early on with no BA experience to point to, but it does not substitute for having actually done the work in this course.
chapters.html:
In short
ECBA (entry) → CCBA (mid) → CBAP (senior); PMI-PBA as an alternative. Demonstrated judgement matters more than the certificate alone.
Part VIII · Chapter 28

Certifications & career paths

A certification can open a door, especially early on with no BA experience to point to, but it does not substitute for having actually done the work in this course.

The common BA certifications
ECBAentry-level, no experience required, good starting signal for a career switcher
CCBAmid-level, requires a couple years of documented BA experience
CBAPsenior-level, requires several years of substantial BA experience
PMI-PBAPMI's equivalent, often favoured in project-heavy, PMI-aligned organizations

A typical path: Business Analyst → Senior Business Analyst → either Product Owner/Product Manager (leaning toward what to build and why) or BA Lead/Practice Lead (leaning toward how a team of BAs works). Neither direction is objectively better, they suit different strengths: product roles reward ownership over outcomes, lead roles reward mentoring and process.

Common trip-ups
  • Collecting certifications before any real experience. Interviewers weigh demonstrated judgement (like the scenarios throughout this course) far more heavily than a certificate alone.
  • Assuming one certification body is universally "the best." Fit depends on your target industry and geography more than any absolute ranking.
28.1

Recap

Certifications signal commitment and baseline knowledge, especially useful early in a career switch, but demonstrated judgement on real scenarios is what actually gets you hired and promoted.

Easy
Which certification is generally most appropriate for someone with no prior BA experience?
Medium
What do interviewers typically weigh most heavily for a BA role?
