chapters.html:
In short
STAR for interview answers: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Most BA interview questions map directly onto a chapter in this course.
Part VIII · Chapter 29

Common BA interview questions & case studies

The last chapter is a rehearsal. Most BA interviews are really testing whether you can apply exactly the judgement this course has been building, out loud, under a little pressure.

The STAR structure for behavioural answers
Situationbrief context, just enough to understand the stakes
Taskwhat you specifically were responsible for
Actionwhat you actually did, the most important part, be specific
Resultwhat happened, ideally with a number or concrete outcome

A common prompt: "Tell me about a time you had to deal with conflicting stakeholder requirements." That is chapter 08, almost word for word, waiting for a STAR-shaped answer built from a real (or realistic) example.

Common trip-ups
  • Vague, generic answers. "I'm good at communication" convinces nobody; a specific situation and a specific action does.
  • Skipping the "Result." An answer without an outcome leaves the interviewer wondering whether the action actually worked.
29.1

Recap

This entire course has been the substance behind good BA interview answers: RACI, root-cause thinking, BRDs and user stories, process modeling, prioritization, and honest stakeholder communication. STAR is just the structure that presents that substance clearly under interview pressure.

Medium
Using STAR, answer: "Tell me about a time (real or realistic) you had to prioritize between conflicting stakeholder requests."
Hard
Case study: a mid-size company's customer support tickets have doubled in 3 months, but nobody knows why. As the BA brought in to investigate, describe your first three concrete steps.

extras.title: Business Analyst interview questions
extras.lead: A deep, topic-by-topic bank of the questions BA interviews actually turn on, grouped by area, with concise answers and the frameworks interviewers want named. Click any question to expand it.
extras.html:
Interview prep

Business Analyst interview questions

A deep, topic-by-topic bank of the questions BA interviews actually turn on, grouped by area, with concise answers and the frameworks interviewers want named. Click any question to expand it.

Role & fundamentals

BeginnerWhat does a Business Analyst actually do?

A BA sits between the people who need something and the people who build it: eliciting the real need, documenting it clearly as requirements, and making sure what gets built solves the problem. They describe the what, not dictate the how.

BeginnerBA vs Product Owner vs Project Manager?

The BA defines and clarifies requirements; the Product Owner owns and prioritises the product backlog and the vision; the Project Manager owns scope, schedule, budget and risk. On small teams one person may wear more than one hat, but the accountabilities differ.

BeginnerFunctional vs non-functional requirements?

Functional: what the system must do ("a user can reset a password"). Non-functional: how well it must do it — performance, security, availability, usability ("pages load under 2 seconds"). Interviewers listen for whether you remember the non-functionals.

BeginnerBRD vs FRD vs SRS?

BRD — high-level business needs and goals. FRD — detailed functional behaviour that satisfies the BRD. SRS — functional plus non-functional and technical detail for the build.

IntermediateWhat makes a good requirement?

Clear, unambiguous, testable, feasible, necessary, consistent with others, and traceable to a business need. If two readers could interpret it differently, it is not done. Describe the need, not a premature solution.

Eliciting requirements

IntermediateHow do you elicit requirements?

Pick a mix: interviews, facilitated workshops (JAD), surveys, observation/job-shadowing, document analysis, and prototyping. Then model and play them back to confirm understanding.

IntermediateInterviews vs workshops?

Interviews go deep with one stakeholder and surface individual detail; workshops bring many stakeholders together to build shared understanding and resolve conflicts quickly. Workshops are efficient but need strong facilitation and preparation.

IntermediateHow do you handle an unavailable or uncooperative stakeholder?

Escalate through the sponsor if needed, but first reduce the ask: use asynchronous methods (short surveys, review-by-email), lean on documents and proxies, and make the cost of their absence visible (decisions blocked, risk logged). Keep them informed so they stay engaged.

IntermediateWhat is the role of prototypes and wireframes?

They turn abstract requirements into something stakeholders can react to, surfacing misunderstandings early and cheaply. "I will know it when I see it" stakeholders especially benefit — a wireframe elicits far more than a paragraph.

AdvancedHow do you validate and verify requirements?

Verification: are we building the requirement right (complete, consistent, testable)? Validation: are these the right requirements (do they meet the business need)? Techniques: reviews/walkthroughs, prototypes, traceability back to objectives, and stakeholder sign-off.

Stakeholders

BeginnerWhat is stakeholder analysis?

Identifying everyone affected by or able to affect the project and planning how to engage them — commonly on a power/interest grid: manage closely (high/high), keep satisfied (high power, low interest), keep informed (low power, high interest), monitor (low/low).

IntermediateWhat is a RACI matrix?

A responsibility grid naming who is Responsible, Accountable, Consulted and Informed for each task, with exactly one Accountable per task.

TaskSponsorBADev teamEnd users
Approve budgetACII
Gather requirementsCRCC
Build the featureICR / AI
User acceptance testingICCR

R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed. Exactly one A per row.

IntermediateHow do you handle conflicting stakeholder requirements?

Surface the conflict early, trace each requirement to a business objective, and facilitate a decision rather than pick sides — using prioritisation (MoSCoW), data, and the accountable owner (sponsor/PO). Document the decision and its trade-off.

IntermediateHow do you tailor communication to different stakeholders?

Executives want outcomes, cost and risk in a page; developers want precise, testable detail; end users want workflows and screens. Match the medium and level of detail to the audience, and confirm understanding rather than assume it.

Documentation & modelling

BeginnerWhat is a user story and what makes a good one?

Format: "As a <role>, I want <goal>, so that <benefit>." A good one follows INVEST (Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable) with clear acceptance criteria.

IntermediateHow do you write good acceptance criteria?

Make them testable and unambiguous; the Gherkin Given/When/Then format works well:

Given an order is out for delivery
When the customer opens the app
Then they see a live status and an ETA
IntermediateUse case vs user story?

A user story is a lightweight reminder to have a conversation; a use case fully describes the actor-system interaction including main, alternate and exception flows. Stories suit Agile; use cases suit detailed up-front analysis.

AdvancedWhich UML diagrams does a BA use?

Most often: use case diagrams (actors and system scope), activity diagrams (workflow/business process), and sequence diagrams (interaction over time). Class/ER diagrams help when modelling data.

IntermediateWhat is as-is vs to-be process mapping?

You map the current ("as-is") process to expose pain points, then design the improved ("to-be") process the solution enables. The gap between them drives the requirements.

As-is process→Analyse gaps→To-be process→Requirements
AdvancedWhat is BPMN?

Business Process Model and Notation — a standard visual language for process flows (events, activities, gateways, swimlanes). Its value is a shared, unambiguous notation that both business and technical readers understand.

AdvancedWhat is an ERD, and why would a BA care?

An Entity-Relationship Diagram models data entities and their relationships (one-to-many, etc.). A BA uses it to agree what data the business needs, its rules and cardinality, before developers design tables.

Prioritisation & scope

BeginnerHow do you prioritise requirements (MoSCoW)?

MoSCoW: Must have, Should have, Could have, Will not have (this time). It forces explicit trade-offs and a shared view of what is essential versus nice-to-have.

IntermediateWhat is the Kano model?

It classifies features by how they affect satisfaction: basic (expected; absence angers), performance (more is better), and delighters (unexpected wins). It helps balance must-haves against differentiators.

IntermediateWhat is scope creep and how do you manage it?

Uncontrolled growth of scope after baseline. Prevent it with a clear signed-off baseline and traceability; manage it with a change-control process where new asks get impact-assessed (cost, time, risk) and approved by the accountable owner. Say yes to change, but make the trade-off explicit.

AdvancedWhat is a requirements traceability matrix?

An RTM links each requirement forward to its design, build and test cases (and back to the business objective). It proves coverage and makes impact analysis easy when something changes.

IntermediateWhat is an MVP?

Minimum Viable Product — the smallest release that delivers real value and lets you learn from users. It is about validated learning and early value, not a half-built product.

Methodologies

IntermediateWaterfall vs Agile?

Waterfall gathers all requirements up front in sequential phases (predictable, rigid). Agile delivers iteratively and welcomes change (adaptive, needs engagement). Choice depends on how well requirements are understood and how much change is expected.

AdvancedWhat is the BA role in Agile / Scrum?

Often working as or with the Product Owner: maintaining and refining the backlog, writing stories and acceptance criteria, clarifying scope during the sprint, and participating in planning, refinement, review and retrospective to keep the team aligned with business value.

IntermediateEpic vs feature vs user story?

An epic is a large body of work spanning many sprints; it breaks into features, which break into user stories small enough to deliver within a sprint. It is a hierarchy of decreasing size and increasing detail.

IntermediateWhat is backlog refinement (grooming)?

An ongoing activity where the team clarifies, estimates, splits and re-prioritises upcoming backlog items so they are "ready" before sprint planning. The BA/PO drives clarity and acceptance criteria.

BeginnerWhat are the phases of the SDLC?

Planning, requirements/analysis, design, development, testing, deployment, and maintenance. Different models (Waterfall, iterative, Agile) sequence or repeat these differently, but the activities recur.

Analysis techniques

IntermediateWhat is gap analysis?

Comparing the current state to the desired state to identify the "gap" — the capabilities, processes or data that must change. The gaps become the requirements/initiatives.

IntermediateWhat is SWOT analysis?

A strategic scan of Strengths, Weaknesses (internal), Opportunities and Threats (external). A BA uses it to frame context and justify why a change is worth doing.

AdvancedHow do you do root-cause analysis?

Get past symptoms to the underlying cause. 5 Whys: ask "why" repeatedly until you reach the root. Fishbone (Ishikawa): categorise possible causes (people, process, tools, etc.) to structure the search. Fixing the root prevents recurrence.

AdvancedWhat is a cost-benefit or feasibility analysis?

A cost-benefit analysis weighs the expected value of a change against its cost (often as ROI/payback). A feasibility study checks whether it is viable technically, operationally and economically before committing. Both support the go/no-go decision.

Delivery, testing & behavioural

IntermediateWhat is the BA role in UAT?

The BA helps define acceptance criteria, plans UAT scenarios from the requirements, supports business users running them, and triages defects — confirming the solution actually meets the documented need before go-live.

IntermediateAcceptance criteria vs test cases?

Acceptance criteria define, in business terms, when a story is done and acceptable. Test cases are the concrete step-by-step checks (inputs, actions, expected results) QA runs to verify those criteria. Criteria are the "what"; test cases are the "how to prove it".

IntermediateDefinition of Ready vs Definition of Done?

Ready: the item is clear, estimated and has acceptance criteria, so the team can start it. Done: the agreed quality bar (built, tested, documented, accepted) for calling work complete. They guard the entry and exit of the sprint.

AdvancedHow do you handle requirements that change mid-project?

Treat change as normal, not failure. Assess impact (scope, cost, time, risk), route it through change control, re-prioritise with the owner, and update the requirements and traceability. In Agile, changes flow through the backlog for the next sprint rather than disrupting the current one.

AdvancedHow do you push back on a stakeholder request you disagree with?

Anchor on the business objective and data, not opinion. Clarify the underlying need (they may be proposing a solution), show the trade-off or evidence, offer an alternative, and let the accountable owner decide. Disagree respectfully, document the decision, and commit.

AdvancedHow would you structure a "tell me about a conflict you resolved" answer?

Use STAR: Situation (context), Task (your responsibility), Action (what you specifically did, emphasising facilitation and data), Result (the outcome, ideally quantified, and what you learned). Keep it concise and centred on your role.

extras.title: Cheat sheet
extras.lead: Every published chapter's core idea on one page.
extras.html:
Quick reference

Cheat sheet

Every published chapter's core idea on one page.

Groundwork

00 What a BA actually does
A BA elicits, documents, and validates — sitting between business needs and the delivery team, translating vague asks into precise, buildable requirements.
0b The BA toolkit landscape
JIRA/Azure DevOps for tracking, Confluence/Notion for documents, Excel for matrices, Visio/draw.io for diagrams, SQL for answering real data questions directly.
0i Meet the company
TastyGo: a food-delivery company (customers, restaurants, orders) making money on commission. Recurring stakeholders: Ops Head, Support Lead, Engineering Manager, Marketing Lead.

Part I · Understanding the business

01 Stakeholders & RACI
RACI: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome, exactly one), Consulted (input before), Informed (told after).
02 Needs vs. wants
5 Whys: keep asking "why" 3-5 times past the first stated want, until you reach an actual, fixable root cause.
03 As-is vs. to-be
Map the as-is (how it works today) before designing the to-be (the improved version) — target only the documented bottleneck.
04 SWOT & feasibility
SWOT: Strengths, Weaknesses (internal) / Opportunities, Threats (external). Feasibility: Technical, Operational, Financial, Schedule.

Part II · Eliciting requirements

05 Elicitation techniques
Elicitation toolbox: interviews (depth), workshops (group agreement), surveys (breadth), observation (say vs. do), document analysis (already half-written).
06 Running a workshop
Workshops: send the agenda ahead, timebox topics and use a parking lot for tangents, send written notes right after.
07 Writing interview questions
Prefer open questions over leading ones ("walk me through..." not "don't you think..."). Ask one thing at a time. Use "why" and "how often" follow-ups.
08 Conflicting stakeholder asks
Conflicting stakeholders: name the conflict openly, find the shared underlying goal, escalate with a specific recommendation, not just the problem.

Part III · Documenting requirements

09 Business Requirements Doc
BRD sections: Purpose & background, Business objectives, Scope & out of scope, Requirements, Assumptions & sign-off. Business language, not technical.
10 Functional Requirements Doc
FRD turns an approved BRD into specific, testable system rules. If a rule can't become a pass/fail test case, it isn't precise enough yet.
11 User stories & acceptance criteria
User story: "As a [role], I want [goal], so that [benefit]." Acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then. Check quality with INVEST.
12 Use cases & diagrams
Use case = actor + goal + main flow + alternate flows. Broader than a user story; alternate flows catch what the happy path misses.

Part IV · Modeling the process

13 Process mapping basics
Process map shapes: oval (start/end), rectangle (step), diamond (decision, 2+ paths out), arrow (sequence).
14 BPMN fundamentals
BPMN: pools/lanes (who), tasks (steps), gateways (exclusive = one path, parallel = all paths), events (start/end/timer).
15 Data flow diagrams
DFD building blocks: external entity (square), process (circle), data store (open rectangle), labelled data flow (arrow). Maps data movement, not sequence.
16 ER modeling for BAs
ER modeling: entity (thing), attribute (property), relationship + cardinality (1:1, 1:M, M:M). M:M relationships usually need a junction entity.

Part V · Analysis techniques

17 Gap analysis
Gap analysis: current state vs. specific, measurable desired state = the gap, which becomes the project's scope.
18 Impact & traceability
Impact analysis: what else does this change touch? Traceability matrix: requirement → design → build → test, nothing falls through.
19 Prioritization (MoSCoW, Kano)
MoSCoW: Must / Should / Could / Won't (this release). Kano: Basic (prevents anger), Performance (scales with investment), Excitement (delights via surprise).
20 Data analysis for BAs
Validate stakeholder claims against real data (GROUP BY / ORDER BY from SQLingo) before treating an impression as fact.

Part VI · Agile & delivery

21 Agile fundamentals for BAs
Agile spreads BA work across the sprint: refine stories ahead of time, support planning, confirm delivered work at review against acceptance criteria.
22 Backlog management
Definition of Ready (gate to enter a sprint) vs. Definition of Done (gate to call it finished). Split oversized stories by value, not by technical layer.
23 Working with UX
Review wireframes against acceptance criteria and requirements; leave visual/styling calls to the designer.
24 UAT & sign-off
UAT test cases translate directly from acceptance criteria. Independent testers plus a named sign-off owner.

Part VII · Communication

25 Stakeholder reporting
RAG status: Green (on track), Amber (at risk, be aware), Red (blocked, needs action). Be honest, match detail to the audience.
26 Managing scope creep
Treat every "small addition" as a change request: log it, assess impact, get an explicit approve/defer/reject decision.
27 Presenting findings
Structure findings as Situation → So what → Recommendation. Lead with the headline and a specific ask for senior audiences.

Part VIII · Career

28 Certifications & career paths
ECBA (entry) → CCBA (mid) → CBAP (senior); PMI-PBA as an alternative. Demonstrated judgement matters more than the certificate alone.
29 Interview questions & cases
STAR for interview answers: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Most BA interview questions map directly onto a chapter in this course.
